ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bolognese pasta; FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest defensible broader class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta dishes — composite cooked dish with meat sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers pasta-based composite dishes; used as the closest defensible category for a pasta dish with meat sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Bolognese pasta dish; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of pasta Bolognese weighs approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g cooked pasta and 150 g meat sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Spaghetti with meat sauce, homemade (SR Legacy ID 21299) and component-level data for cooked pasta and beef meat sauce
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 185.71 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 8.57 g, carbohydrate 17.14 g) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy entry for spaghetti with meat sauce (approximately 182–190 kcal/100 g, protein 9–11 g, fat 7–9 g, carbohydrate 16–18 g). Values retained from input and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Secondary micronutrient values (calcium, iron, sodium, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) also cross-validated against USDA data and found plausible. Potassium estimated from USDA component data for tomato-based meat sauce and pasta (~280 mg/100 g). Extended micronutrients (vitamins, minerals beyond those reported in input) are not reliably available at the composite dish level in USDA SR Legacy for this preparation and are set to -1 per G3 sentinel rules.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for spaghetti with meat sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of approximately 45–52 for spaghetti-based pasta dishes with meat sauce (white pasta, al dente). A midpoint GI of 47 is used as the best defensible analogous estimate for Pasta Bolognese made with refined white pasta cooked al dente. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 17.14 g/100 g × 350 g serving / 100) = (47 × 59.99 g) / 100 ≈ 28.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 271 mg/100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, representing 41% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request preparation with reduced-sodium broth, no added salt, minimal or no Parmesan garnish, and omission of red wine to keep single-meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined white pasta contributes 17.1 g carbohydrate/100 g with only 1.7 g dietary fibre, yielding a GI of approximately 47 (low-medium boundary) but a high glycemic load of ~28 per 350 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute wholegrain or legume-based pasta to increase fibre and reduce glycemic load, limit portion size to 200 g or less, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.43 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.14 g/100 g from minced beef; a 350 g serving provides approximately 12 g saturated fat, approaching the AHA daily limit of 13 g for a 2,000 kcal diet.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request lean minced beef or substitute with turkey mince, limit portion to 200–250 g, avoid Parmesan garnish, and ensure preparation without butter to keep saturated fat intake within AHA guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine pathways; tomato-based sauce contains nightshade compounds that may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting minced beef with minced turkey or lentils, monitor for symptom flares from tomato-based sauce, and request preparation without red wine to avoid alcohol-related interactions with RA medications such as methotrexate.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasta Bolognese (also known as Alla Bolognese, Spaghetti Bolognese, or Ragù alla Bolognese) is a classic Italian pasta dish originating from Bologna in the Emilia-Romagna region of Italy. It consists of al dente pasta — traditionally tagliatelle or spaghetti — tossed in a rich, slow-cooked meat sauce (ragù) made from minced beef, tomatoes, tomato paste, onion, carrot, celery, garlic, olive oil, and optionally red wine and beef broth, finished with fresh basil, oregano, and grated Parmesan cheese. The sauce is simmered for one to three hours to develop deep umami flavour and a thick, glossy texture. The dish has a savoury, slightly sweet, and mildly acidic flavour profile with a tender, meaty texture. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 186 kcal, 10 g protein, 8.6 g fat (3.4 g saturated), and 17.1 g carbohydrate with 1.7 g dietary fibre and 271 mg sodium. A standard restaurant serving of 350 g delivers approximately 650 kcal and 950 mg sodium. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium load, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to refined pasta glycemic load (~28 per serving), and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from minced beef. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those on gluten-free diets. In Singapore, it is commonly served in Italian restaurants and casual dining chains as a popular Western pasta option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in spaghetti Bolognese?
What is the glycemic index of pasta Bolognese?
Is spaghetti Bolognese good for weight loss?
How much protein in pasta Bolognese?
What pasta is used in Bolognese?
Can I eat Bolognese with hypertension?
